mnemonics: FETCH-DECODE-EXECUTE-STORE: The fundamental instruction cycle, like a chef getting a recipe (FETCH), reading it (DECODE), cooking (EXECUTE), and serving (STORE).
keyPoints: The CPU fetches an instruction from memory, decodes it to determine the required action, executes the operation, and may store the result back to memory or a register.
The Program Counter (PC) holds the memory address of the next instruction to be executed.
Registers are small, high-speed storage locations inside the CPU used to hold data and addresses currently being processed.
commonMistakes: Confusing the Memory Address Register (MAR) with the Memory Data Register (MDR). The MAR holds the address to read/write; the MDR holds the actual data from/to that address.
Assuming all instructions take the same number of clock cycles to execute. Simple arithmetic might take one cycle, while a memory access could take many more.
